title: Regression: Predicting a Number
concept: Regression is the supervised learning task of predicting a continuous number, such as a price, a temperature, or an age, rather than a category.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which of these best describes a regression task?

options.text: Predicting a number, like a price or a temperature
Sorting data into a fixed set of categories
Writing rules by hand for a computer to follow

exercises.explain: Right! Regression is all about predicting a number.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Classification and regression always produce the same kind of output.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Classification outputs a category; regression outputs a number — they're different kinds of output.

cards.title: Guess my age
cards.body: Imagine a carnival game: "Guess my age!" You don't pick from a list like "kid, teen, or adult" — you guess an actual number, like 34. That specific-number guessing is exactly what regression does.

cards.title: What regression actually predicts
cards.body: Regression predicts a continuous number — a value that can be anything along a scale, including decimals, not just a handful of fixed options.

cards.title: A number line, not a checklist
cards.body: Think of regression like a number line stretching in both directions. The prediction can land anywhere on that line — 72.4°F, $1,299,000, or 8 years old — instead of picking from a short checklist of options.

cards.title: Three everyday number guesses
cards.body: A weather app predicts temperature (like 81.6°F). A real-estate app predicts price (like $415,000). A photo app estimates someone's age from a picture (like 27). All three are regression — the answer is a number.

cards.title: The number doesn't have to be exact
cards.body: You might assume a regression prediction must be perfectly correct. In reality, it's an estimate — a weather app might predict 81.6°F when it's actually 80°F. Being close counts; regression is about getting near the right number, not hitting it exactly.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is an example of a regression prediction?

options.text: Estimating a car's resale value in dollars
Sorting emails into inbox or spam
Tagging a photo as "beach" or "mountain"

exercises.explain: Right! A dollar value is a continuous number, which makes this a regression task.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Regression predictions must always be whole numbers, like 5 or 10, never decimals.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Regression outputs can include decimals, like 5.7 or 72.4 — that's what makes them continuous.

exercises.sentence: Because a regression output can be any value along a scale, we call it a ___ number.
exercises.explain: Continuous means the value can smoothly take on any point on a scale, including decimals.

exercises.prompt: Which of these outputs was most likely produced by a regression model?

options.text: Predicted age: 29.3 years
Pet type: Dog
Support ticket topic: Billing

exercises.explain: 29.3 is a specific number on a continuous scale, so it's a regression output.

exercises.prompt: A fitness tracker estimates how many calories you burned on a run. Why is this a regression task?

options.text: Because calories burned is a number that can fall anywhere on a scale
Because there are only two possible answers
Because it sorts your run into a category
Because calories can only be whole numbers

exercises.explain: Exactly! Calories burned could be 312, 312.5, or any other number — a job for regression.

exercises.prompt: An airline wants to predict how many minutes a flight will be delayed (it could be 0, 12, or 47 minutes, etc). Is this best framed as regression, and why?

options.text: Yes, because delay time is a number that can take many possible values
No, because delays should be sorted into "on-time" or "late" only
No, because minutes aren't real numbers
Yes, but only because airlines care about money

exercises.explain: Right! Delay time can be almost any number of minutes, which is exactly what regression is built for.

exercises.prompt: A tutoring app predicts a student's score out of 100 on a practice test (e.g., 87). A different feature just labels each student as "passing" or "failing". Which statement is correct?

options.text: Predicting the score is regression; labeling pass/fail is classification
Both are regression because they both involve test performance
Both are classification because both are about students
Predicting the score is classification because 100 is a fixed number

exercises.explain: Exactly! A numeric score is regression, while a pass/fail label is classification — same test, two different task types.
